sentences.text: Good morning.
My name is Rose Schwartz, maiden name, Halpert, born in [?
Velka ?] Krive, Czechoslovakia.
Kruve, ?] it's K-R-U-V-E.
And-- and-- I don't know.
Do you need the address?
Just part of it.
We lived in 24 [NON-ENGLISH] Podkarpatska Rus.
And I remember it was a very nice town.
We were very happy.
We had about 80 Jewish families.
They were all spread around.
Every family had their own home, their own little business.
And we all had fields and animals.
We had-- we were growing our own vegetables.
We had fruits growing.
We had all the vegetables that we needed for the winter, we're able to use.
My father was a--
My father was Martin, called Mordche, Mordche Halpert.
He was a businessman.
And we were all doing-- we were a very big family.
And each of-- each of the families had their own little business.
But my grandfather tried to keep us together.
And every Saturday night, they used to sit down after [NON-ENGLISH], and have all the business.
I was a little girl, and I was allowed to sit in my father's lap and listen what's going on business-wise.
Sunday was another day.
I come from a very religious family.
They were Orthodox.
My grandfather wore a long black coat with white stockings and [NON-ENGLISH].
So ?] the other ones had similar, but no white-- no long white coat.
[INTERPOSING VOICES].
It was special for my grandfather.
And the-- the few older men wore the white coat-- the long black coat with the white stockings.
My grandfather's name was Joseph Gluck.
And from what I remember, he was born in Poland.
He came over and married in.
My grandmother's name was Sura Rivka Berkovich.
And the family was something-- 15 or 16 children.
I cannot recall their names.
I know a few of them who lived in town.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
My mother's name was Etel.
Etel Perl.
Used to call her Etia Perl Halpert.
And we were seven children.
Their name was Brana, Kreindel.
I was the third one.
Then I had Sarah and Gitel.
Then we had two brothers, which was Isaack and Shimka.
Unfortunately, they were all gone.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When-- when the war started in 1938, we were very protective.
Our parents protected us.
They didn't let us know what was happening really.
But I was-- I was nosy.
And I overheard my father talking to my mother about the war in Germany.
Where we grew up, we didn't have any radios.
So that's all what we had is the newspaper and the public information.
But they didn't let us know what was really happening in the world locally.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
So we heard about the war.
My parents sat down and talked to us.
The war is happening, what's going on.
And they then [? met ?] at the First World War, and the Russian were coming in.
They send away all the young people.
Because once the Russians came in, they took them away to-- to Russia.
So they thought they would do the same thing with us.
Since we had a big family, we will try to separate you, give everybody-- [SOBS] a chance to survive.
Well, I was 12 years when I was send away.
Where did they send you away?
First I went away to my aunt's house, which was also in the same town but the other end of town.
I was staying with her, being the men were already taken away.
It was already war.
This goes back to 1938.
The war-- they took away the men to work camp.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH] in Hungary.
[NON-ENGLISH], a work camp.
So I went out, and I stayed with her.
She had four children.
I helped her out.
I was--
My father, my uncle, two of my uncles, they were taken away.
And most of the men, the eligible men from town, were taken away.
[SIGHS] No-- he came back.
They just took him to work.
I think must have been [? around ?] 1939, but I'm not sure.
I don't remember.
It was like mobilized.
They had to go back, like, to the army.
But they took him away to work.
See, where I was born in town was a very turmoil during the war.
I went to school in Ukraine, and we had Ukrainian schools.
And being that we belong to Czechoslovakia, we learned twice a week Czech.
Then came in-- I think it was 1938, I'm not sure-- a siege came in, which they were Russian-- Russian fighters.
I don't know, probably.
I tried to look it up in the books, and I couldn't find out enough about it.
I don't-- well, it was somehow in the spring.
But they didn't last very long, maybe for about a year or so.
Then Hungary came-- came in.
We were right in the middle of war.
Between the siege and Hungary, we were just out of school for a year.
I missed out an awful lot.
Just--
Well, we-- we had other land, and we worked in the land.
And we helped around the house, nothing special.
We tried to study as much as we could.
The young-- the men, all the Hebrew teachers, were taken away.
They brought in a young student, a yeshiva [? bocher ?] to teach the boys, but he would not-- would not teach the girls.
So whatever I learned in Hebrew, my mother used to sit with me.
When everyone went to bed, she was sitting and teaching me how to read and how to write.
It was a very tough time.
A public school, public school, Ukrainian.
And then we switched to-- to Hungarian when Hungary came over.
I must have been in the fifth grade, they took over.
And we lost about a year.
We went into school.
We did not speak a word of English, and the teachers did not speak a-- a word of Ukrainian.
So we studied.
We tried to understand, which I learned the language at least.
This is a plus.
Ukrainian.
A little bit of Czech and Hungarian--
--we learned.
And of course, I [? started off with Yiddish ?], which was normal.
No, we spoke Yiddish.
We spoke Yiddish.
In the home-- in the house we spoke Yiddish.
Outside we spoke Ukrainian.
So right away, we-- we had two languages to start with.
And when I was by my aunt's-- in-- in my aunt's house, I still had one year of school.
To go.
So I went from there.
And then it-- the war got to be too close.
Pesel, Pesel Berkovich.
And his name was Simon Laizer, and her name was Pesel Berkovich.
And they--
It was still the same town, the same school.
We had a small town.
We only had like two schools.
So there was no problem going to school.
All right.
The pograms already had started.
And from what we knew about it is they might come into one part of the town and take it one day.
Then the next day, or the next month, whenever they decided, they came to the other town.
So--
Oh, we always had anti-Semitic.
But I didn't understand when they used to tell it-- [UKRAINIAN].
That's in Ukrainian-- Jew go to-- to Palestine.
But I was a kid, and we didn't know about it, not enough.
The friends we were playing with.
We had friends.
But we had-- we-- we grew-- I grew up in a mixed-- mixed people.
The Jews had most of the-- all the stores.
They had land.
We had-- it was a very rich community.
The people there owned a lot of land.
I mean, not-- not the Jewish people, but the gentiles.
And we worked with them.
Like, in the springtime, my father could go and buy up-- after the blooming, buy up some fruit.
And then, of course, in the fall we shipped it.
The same thing when the-- with sending away some sheep.
They used to call us [NON-ENGLISH], which in the mountains.
We rented the sheep, and we hired a shepherd.
And the shepherds, they made cheese.
So by the end of the-- the end of the [INAUDIBLE], in the fall, we had a cheese to sell, which was [? extra. ?] It was part of a business.
Pardon me?
The cheeses?
Well, ?] they were just-- they were-- they were-- they sold it.
No, no, we didn't.
But some of the cheeses don't require refrigeration.
And in the fall it was already cold.
And then they came back, maybe they had-- they had the cheese done.
Maybe there were refrigerators, not as far as I remember.
To keep something fresh, my grandfather had a big basement.
And it was funny, because there was a little water running through it, a little brook.
So if he had something extra, we always put it in my grandfather's basement.
But we didn't have any.
We put-- we had everything fresh.
There were fresh produce, fresh milk, fresh things.
The wintertime we saved the fruits from the summer.
And I don't know if you ever heard of it, they used to put away the potatoes, the beets, outside.
It was put in underground and covered it up with the ground.
And you took it out-- in the winter you opened up, and you took it out, and it was fine.
In fact, that helped me to survive in the camps.
Because when we were in Germany, that's what happened.
We were stealing [LAUGHS] the beets and the potatoes.
So--
Well-- well, in Germany?
We were eating it.
We're eating it raw.
We were [? saving-- ?] this--
I don't remember.
I really don't.
So many things which I just can't-- can't remember what was happening.
But then it happened.
The war came closer.
And my mother had a friend, which they went to school.
She married somebody in Kosice, which that was Slovakai.
Kosice, Slovakai.
She send me away to stay with her.
After-- after my aunt.
This is something-- I lost, like, six to eight years.
I can't remember.
If you will ask me how long I stayed here, how long I stay there, I-- I can't.
I've been thinking of it.
I just don't remember if I stood here two years, and then another three, whatever.
I don't remember.
So being I was there, I wasn't too happy.
I made my best-- best of it.
Because she had-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] bless you-- she had two children.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And her husband was also taken away to-- taken away to-- to [NON-ENGLISH], and she had to work.
She was working in a poorhouse for-- where there were children, the-- how do you call it?
Orphanage.
So she took me along with her, and I was working with her in the kitchen.
She taught me quite a bit how to cook, how to bake.
It was pleasant.
She was very nice to me.
I don't know what the arrangement was between my parents and her, how the money was going, because I never saw a penny of it.
I mean, they never paid me for my work or anything.
But she helped me out to buy some new clothes.
She gave me money to go to the movies with her children.
She had two children, and they--
I-- one was Mendel.
The other one was-- I don't remember her name.
But the-- the boy was around my age.
The girl was a little bit younger.
Which-- it was a little sad, because they were going to school, and I had to work.
It-- she treated me very well.
I can't complain.
I kept in touch with my parents.
They used to send-- send me packages, fresh fruit, which there we wouldn't get it, of course.
Everyone enjoyed it.
The worst thing was when I received a letter from my elder sister.
And she says, Rose, [SIGHS] don't cry anymore.
We are all right, but we are not home.
And I didn't know what the letter meant till after the war.
After the war, I first found out what happened.
While I was away, she got married.
So she lived in a different part, which I think was [NON-ENGLISH], I'm not sure that's [INAUDIBLE].
And so my younger sister was with them.
My parents took the whole family and were hidden.
We also owned property in the mountains.
So we knew somebody who hid my parents, which that's where the tragedy start.
My parents decided they wanted to have the family together.
[INAUDIBLE] there was-- they wrote somehow with messenger.
And they would have [INAUDIBLE], and they stayed over.
We had like a bungalow in the country, which we had there a woman and a daughter who took care of our land.
And in the summer, we used to send away there the-- the cows, and she took care of them.
They slept over on the-- on the attic.
But it's a small town, and word got [? along. ?] They-- there was called a siege.
When it was already-- I don't know it was the Hungarian or the Germans came in.
They were looking-- they were bounty hunters-- and they were looking for any Jews.
Whoever brought in, they got a nice reward.
Well, it was a very big story [INAUDIBLE], because somebody was caught.
Because that's all what they were carrying is money and jewelry, and they were killed for the money and jewelry.
But my older sister died on the spot.
My younger one was-- had a knife through her neck, but she didn't-- she didn't die.
She went into town, and from what I understand, they stripped her naked.
She went down to our neighbor, and they give her a sheet to cover herself.
Of course, they called the police.
And they knew the people who killed her.
The people were arrested.
But it cost my family's life.
My younger sister was taken to SIghet, Romania to the hospital, and they sewed her throat together.
And my whole family gave themself up just to give my sister a regular [SOBS] [? burial. ?] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a very sad-- sad time.
And of course, they were taken to Auschwitz after that.

They came into town, and they gave themselves up to the Germans-- allowed them to bury my sister and give her a regular [? burial. ?] And my sister was in the hospit-- when she was ready, they send her to Auschwitz.
From the hospital.
And they send the whole family.
And she was so smart.
She was the younger one.
I met her in Stutthof, and ask her what happened to the family.
It was in the war.
Yes.
And that was in Stutthof.
And I had asked her, what happened to the family-- all of them?
So she told me that my younger sister took a-- took a little baby in her arm to go with the-- with the mother, you know, because the others [INAUDIBLE] are separated.
My [INTERPOSING VOICES]
Together was my mother, Etia Perl.
My sister name was Kreindel and Sarah.
Sarah was the one who her throat is cut.
The other one was Gitel.
How they separated them in Auschwitz, I have no idea, because I don't know the end of the-- I don't know the whole story, what was happening.
In--
--Stutthof, in Stutthof, like two years later.
Two years later, I met her there.
So--
She told me that she was together with my older sister Kreindel.
Somehow when you got off the train in Auschwitz-- I'm jumping ahead-- when you got off a train in Auschwitz, they separated you.
If you were able to work, they put you on one line.
If you have little children and older people, they put a separate line.
I mean, I'm talking from my-- my own experience.
And the men went the same thing.
Neither-- neither of them did.
I lost them all.
On the truck was Kreindel, my older one.
The two older one-- one was dead, and one was taken away.
My younger one, I found out that she was in one camp.
I almost got killed, because when I came into camp, to Stutthof, I saw my sister by the wire, and I ran to her.
And the friends-- while you're away, you meet friends-- don't go.
You will get killed.
But I talked to her, and I arranged I was going to exchange it.
I had one of the girls that was friend of mine, and her sister was in the same camp.
And the-- at night, we were going to exchange them.
She was going to come with me, and the other sister was going to get there.
By the door, there were, like, kapos, our own-- our own Jewish people, and they said they would let me-- let us do it.
But as fate had it, by the time I came back, when I arranged everything, there was an appell.
And somebody covered for me, because the count wasn't there.
But in half an hour later, we left.
If we had stood there one more night, she would have been with me.
I don't know we would have survived, whatever.
[INAUDIBLE]
Friend.
In Kosice--
--now.
In the kitchen.
I was in the kitchen and the dining room helping out--
--whatever it was necessary.
First--
First being the-- we were the last ones to go into the ghetto.
The people--
Kosice ghetto.
The people were-- we were like a transit camp.
The people who came over from Europe and the-- from the underground.
They stood there overnight, over a few days, and they were taken away.
They were sent to Israel.
So they-- the government what-- were the officials.
The-- this was still not in the ghetto yet, before we went to the ghetto.
We were in the [INAUDIBLE].
They picked up-- there was always the underground.
People from Poland escaped.
We-- [? and there ?] are young kids who came up at the sewers.
That was in Kosice ghetto-- in Kosice, where I was in Kosice working in the children's home.
I was still living there.
I was still living-- still living in the home, which it went on for quite a bit.
They came, and they went to Israel.
Well--
We-- they did not want to talk too much, because they were afraid.
What they kept, it was everything hush, hush.
So they did not want call name-- tell names or their names.
As a matter of fact, they-- one of the groups stood over.
And I heard the most beautiful song, which it stuck with me.
We had a concert.
And there was a-- he must have been 12 or 13, and he was studying music.
And there was a young kid, and he sung-- he sang this song.
He gave us-- of course, we all cried.
At that time, I didn't understand as much.
Now the words are something.
I would like-
--to record it.
Well--
It went like this.
[NON-ENGLISH SINGING] [SOBBING]
OK.
